items.q: Why is injection moulding rarely used for one-off products?
items.correct: Because the machined steel mould tool is extremely expensive; the cost only becomes economic when spread across thousands or millions of identical mouldings.
items.misconception: Injection moulding is a cheap process whatever the quantity being made.
items.random_wrong: Injection moulding only works with metals, not plastics.
items.why: Students see cheap plastic products and assume the process is cheap; the high fixed tooling cost versus low unit cost trade-off is the key economic concept.

items.q: What is the difference between milling and turning?
items.correct: In turning the workpiece rotates on a lathe against a fixed cutting tool; in milling a rotating cutter removes material from a workpiece held on a table.
items.misconception: Milling and turning are two names for the same machining process.
items.random_wrong: Turning means flipping a part over to machine its underside.
items.why: Both are subtractive machining, so students merge them; remembering what rotates — workpiece (turning) or cutter (milling) — separates them cleanly.

items.q: Can thermosetting plastics be remelted and remoulded like thermoplastics?
items.correct: No — thermosets form irreversible cross-links when cured, so they char rather than melt; only thermoplastics can be reheated and reshaped or recycled by melting.
items.misconception: All plastics can be melted down and remoulded into new products.
items.random_wrong: Thermosets melt at lower temperatures than thermoplastics.
items.why: Students treat 'plastic' as one material; the cross-linking difference determines which processes and recycling routes each class of polymer allows.

items.q: Which process is best suited to producing millions of identical plastic casings?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Injection moulding. “Sand casting” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: On which machine is turning carried out?
items.why: Correct answer: A lathe. “A milling machine” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Continuous lengths with a constant cross-section
items.why: It's Continuous lengths with a constant cross-section. “Hollow blow-moulded bottles” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: A heated thermoplastic sheet. “Molten metal in a crucible” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An injection mould tool costs 40000 pounds. Spread over 100000 mouldings, what is the tooling cost per unit?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.4 £. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
